title: OpenAI offers a public stake as Washington drafts AI rules - TCR 07/02/26
description:
OpenAI pitched giving the US government a 5% equity stake as the White House nears a voluntary frontier-AI standards deal with a classified safety benchmark, US finance and information sectors are shedding 28,000 jobs a month, and a University of Minnesota team built SpudCell, the first synthetic cell assembled from a 36-gene genome that feeds, grows, and divides.

https://sharedsapience.com/the-century-report-july-2-2026/

0:00 The Big AI settlement and OpenAI's 5% stake
0:44 The AI jobs data refuses the simple story
3:39 The AI answer layer grows its first fixtures
6:21 SpudCell and synthetic biology's leap
9:24 Data-center power bills get a name
12:28 Tollbooths on the Answer Layer
14:48 Synthesis and Closing

This episode covers the emerging federal Big AI settlement and OpenAI's 5% stake pitch, the FTC warning on state AI laws, the AI labor picture and the 30% rehire projection, the SpudCell synthetic-cell breakthrough, New Jersey's data-center tariff bill and Henrico County's 25% rate hike, Etched's working inference chip, and the AI answer layer's new tollbooths and harm-reporting tools.

Washington's handling of frontier AI is consolidating from one-off gates into a durable settlement. The administration and Anthropic, OpenAI, Amazon, Microsoft, and Google are expected to announce shared frontier-model standards vetted through Commerce's AI center and the NSA, while OpenAI floats a 5% federal equity stake modeled on Alaska's Permanent Fund and the FTC warns that following state AI laws could trigger Section 5 enforcement. The same days argued the fence may not hold: Kate Adamala's Minnesota team assembled SpudCell from 36 genes and open-sourced the recipe through the nonprofit Biotic, Etched shipped a working inference chip with over $1 billion in orders, and open-weight models kept closing the gap. On labor, finance and information payrolls are falling 28,000 a month even as Gartner projects up to 30% of AI-cut roles rehired by 2029 and Stanford's Digital Economy Lab separates automation from augmentation. New Jersey sent its governor a tariff bill binding data centers above 50MW to pay their own grid cost, retroactive to existing facilities.
